data.title: Arcutis Biotherapeutics: Zoryve's Growth Impresses, But Valuation Is A Concern (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Arcutis Biotherapeutics (NASDAQ:ARQT) has been on a rollercoaster since my “Sell” rating in Summer 2023.

The stock dropped nearly 80% in the months following my last look - only to rally back and then some.

The company markets Zoryve (roflumilast), a topical phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitor, for the treatment of plaque psoriasis, mild to moderate atopic dermatitis, and seborrheic dermatitis. It is also awaiting FDA approval for scalp and body psoriasis.

While I acknowledged roflumilast’s utility in plaque psoriasis, I cautioned that other investigational indications, such as seborrheic dermatitis, would “contend with intense competition.” I also expressed some concern over Arcutis’ high operating expenses.

Judging by the stock’s outperformance last year, and so far this year, Arcutis must have made notable progress. Let’s take a look!

Recent Developments

When I first visited Arcutis, Zoryve was generating no more than $5 million per quarter and Arcutis was burning (cash from operations) $60 million to $80 million every quarter. The company’s financial statements have since changed.

Revenues skyrocketed over 470% year-over-year in 2024 to $196.5 million. In Q4 2024, total revenues were $71.36 million with Zoryve generating $69.4 million of that. Cost of revenues was $6.9 million. SG&A expenses were $57.6 million. Notably, revenue growth is really outpacing SG&A expenses. The latter has, essentially, stalled.

This signals that Zoryve is generating a lot of interest on its own merit in the plaque psoriasis market. The net loss in Q4 was $7.6 million. This was much improved from a net loss of $61.2 million in Q4 2023. Impressively, Arcutis utilized only $0.7 million in operating activities. So, it is very close to generating cash.

Arcutis recognizes revenue from Zoryve product sales when its customer (pharmaceutical wholesalers and specialty pharmacy providers) obtains control (typically upon delivery). Its customers can return Zoryve. In Q4, there was a $4.1 million true-up impact to product revenue. When Zoryve was launched, the company estimated likely returns based on industry data. Because fewer products were being returned than initially planned, it had to make the $4.1 million adjustment. So, this is a positive sign as it implies that its customers are having no problem selling Zoryve.

Depending on the source, peak annual estimates for Zoryve range between $800 million and $3.8 billion. This is likely a result of the many markets Zoryve is pursuing. Psoriasis is the largest opportunity and Arcutis competes with biologics like Humira, non-injectable systemic therapies like Otezla, and topical therapies like tapinarof. Notably, Zoryve is the only topical phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitor approved for psoriasis and there doesn’t appear to be any class competition on the horizon.

The largest question mark is the IP strength for Zoryve. Because its active ingredient (roflumilast) had been previously approved (in 2011 as an oral tablet for plaque psoriasis). Zoryve was granted only a three-year exclusivity period upon its approval. This prevents the FDA from approving generic versions of the drug. However, this exclusivity period for plaque psoriasis ends in July and there are already a couple of companies “knocking at the door.” Back in March 2024, Padagis Israel Pharmaceuticals Ltd. submitted an Abbreviated New Drug Application, or ANDA, for a generic version of Zoryve cream 0.3% (plaque psoriasis). Arcutis followed with a patent infringement lawsuit, claiming that the ANDA breaches key patents that last through 2037. Earlier this week, a “pause” agreement was reached between Arcutis and Padagis that pushes back the timeline for potential generic competition for Zoryve. This, essentially, buys Arcutis more time with market exclusivity in the event Padagis’ generic is approved, pending future litigation outcomes. In Europe, Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) is challenging two of Arcutis’ topical roflumilast patents. This is developing.

Financial Health

Turning to the balance sheet, as of December 31, Arcutis held $71.335 million in cash and cash equivalents. Marketable securities totaled $156.62 million. Total current assets were $335.82 million, while total current liabilities were $81.013 million. So, Arcutis has solid liquidity in the short-term. The company does owe $107.23 million in long-term debt (principal is $100 million).

Again, cash burn was negligible in Q4, so its runway should extend well into the future.

Valuation Assumptions

Arcutis’ $1.8 billion enterprise value is built on assumptions of future cash flows. I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to uncover implicit expectations for revenue growth and profitability.

As for inputs, analyst estimates for 2025 call for $300 million in revenue. This will serve as “Year 1” in the model. 25% revenue growth serves as an average throughout the projection period. This may not seem that high at first, but achieving 25% year-over-year revenue growth for nine years is no small task. A free cash flow margin of 20% represents a realistic long-term goal for a pharmaceutical company. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, we have Arcutis eclipsing $1 billion in 2031.

Even with these optimistic assumptions, Arcutis is marginally overvalued. As I noted earlier, Zoryve’s label is expanding. In addition, the company has a number of pipeline candidates, but most are preclinical (several years out). So, for my purposes, Arcutis is Zoryve.

Next, my sensitivity analysis reveals how adjustments to revenue growth and FCF margins impact intrinsic valuation. It also reveals what the market might be pricing Arcutis’ stock for.

For simplicity, it appears the market is pricing Arcutis for greater than 25% year-over-year revenue growth and 20% FCF margins.

Lastly, my Monte Carlo simulation runs 10,000 iterations of the DCF model. Each iteration randomly varies key inputs like stock beta, growth, and margins. This gives us a more realistic and dynamic look at outcomes.

While Arcutis is a bit overvalued (based on my inputs), it is priced within reason (under the 95th percentile per share value of $20.06). Because Arcutis is priced for a lot of success already, its risk/reward profile skews towards the downside.

ARQT Stock: Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, Zoryve’s launch is going exceptionally well and the drug appears to be popular among prescribers and patients. Arcutis is nearing cash flow positivity and continuing to expand Zoryve’s label. That being said, there are some notable risks here. For starters, while patents could protect Zoryve from generic launches until 2037, it is not guaranteed. After all, roflumilast is not a novel drug and Arcutis will likely have to fight like a dog to keep Zoryve from succumbing to generics. This isn’t my main issue, though, and patent litigation outcomes are always unpredictable. My main issue is Arcutis’ valuation, which seems to be priced to perfection. So while ARQT warrants a rating upgrade to “Hold,” I’m still holding off on buying pending better entry prices. However, I can certainly see ARQT’s utility in a barbell portfolio, especially as there is still plenty of room for upside even if the company meets expectations. Finally, as Arcutis is dependent on Zoryve for most its revenues, any negative developments regarding Zoryve could cause a significant and instantaneous drop in Arcutis' valuation. So, it'd be wise to be aware of this risk.

data.title: Deere: A Question Of Fiscal Support
data.description:
Deere's (NYSE:DE) share price remains near all-time highs, despite soft fundamentals and a growing threat from tariffs. I find this somewhat surprising as I have been expecting weakness to drag Deere's stock significantly lower.

Investors are anticipating a rapid return to growth and margin improvements, but I continue to believe that this is overly optimistic. Recent poor performance has been driven by normalization after a boom rather than a cycle trough. In addition, lower farm income, elevated used inventories and tariffs all represent threats to Deere's sales and margins.

Market Conditions

Across end markets, Deere continues to face headwinds from high rates and macro uncertainty, a situation that is likely to be exacerbated by tariffs. Deere expects Ag equipment sales in North America to decline around 30% in 2025. In addition to high rates and macro uncertainty, elevated used inventory levels are also weighing on demand. In addition, farm income remains under pressure, but Deere appears to be counting on government support to largely offset this. The USDA is projecting that US crop cash receipts will be fairly flat in 2025. Income is expected to be up over 20% though due to government support. Where this situation ends up with uncertainty regarding tariffs and a government focused on reducing spending is highly uncertain, though. As a result, the extent to which the government is willing to support farm income and whether this is directed towards equipment sales will be an important determinant of Deere’s sales in coming quarters.

Small Ag and Turf sales in North America are also expected to be down around 10% in 2025. While the fundamentals of the dairy and livestock segment are strong at the moment, this isn’t leading to equipment purchases. If this holds across the entire ag segment, a rebound in farm income may not lead to improved sales.

Deere believes that fundamentals are solid within Construction and Forestry. Despite this, demand is weak, which has been attributed to interest rates, uncertainty and competition. There is also a risk that the demand environment deteriorates significantly due to a rapidly building over supply in the new housing market, which could lead to an extended period of depressed construction activity.

Construction and Forestry industry equipment sales in North America are expected to decline 5-10% in 2025. While many end markets within this segment are softening, US Government infrastructure spending remains elevated and only around 50% of ILJA funds have been committed.

Tariffs

While Deere is a major manufacturer in the US, and a net exporter of ag and turf equipment, the company will be negatively impacted by tariffs. This is due to the expected impact of tariffs on farm incomes, rising input costs and potential reciprocal tariffs.

Tariffs will increase the cost of inputs like fertilizer and could weigh on exports if other countries pursue retaliatory tariffs. Roughly 85% of the potash used in agriculture in the US is imported from Canada. Canada also supplies around 25% of the US' nitrogen fertilizer demand. China has threatened 10-15% tariffs on US agricultural goods, which is significant given that China imported close to 30 billion USD of agricultural products from the US in 2024. The trade war with China cost US farmers an estimated 27 billion USD in 2018. Farmers received around 23 billion USD in support from the federal government though.

While over 75% of the units Deere sells in the US are assembled domestically, the company has suggested that its COGS could rise by something like 5-10%. Any reciprocal tariffs could also impact Deere's sales in international markets. Large export markets for the company include Canada, Europe, Brazil and Australia. A strong dollar is also a headwind for exporters, which would impact Deere through both its own sales internationally and by reducing farm income in the US.

Financial Analysis

Deere’s revenue declined 30% YoY in Q1, with production and precision ag net sales down 37% and small ag and turf net sales down 28%. Shipping volumes were lower than expected in Q4, although Deere believes that it will recover this shortfall over the course of the year. Timing of shipments and currency translation were also headwinds during the quarter.

Deere is expecting 5.0-5.5 billion USD net income in FY25, along with 4.5-5.5 billion USD operating cash flow and around 1.6 billion USD CapEx. This outlook will be impacted by tariffs though and appears to be based on an assumption of improving demand as the year progresses. It is not really clear where this optimism comes from though given that Deere's early order program was soft and data from AEM also points to continued weakness.

In addition, Tractor Zoom data seems to suggest that used inventory remains a problem, which is not surprising given the surge in equipment sales in recent years. Deere has suggested that used inventory levels have improved in recent months. Used inventory levels remain elevated in North America though and Deere is focused on rectifying this situation.

Currency could also be a headwind in coming quarters. As a result of these headwinds, I believe that Deere's net income could come in closer to 4 billion USD in FY25.

Deere's gross margins continue to decline as its sales fall. Margins have actually held up reasonably well though, all things considered. Planned underproduction has been helping to reduce field inventory levels. This is presumably a headwind to margins at the moment though. Under production was particularly acute within Construction and Forestry. Deere's large Ag field inventory was down 25% YoY at the end of 2024 and now sits below pre-COVID levels. Reduced inventory is supportive of pricing though, despite the soft demand environment. I question how long Deere can continue pushing higher pricing in an environment of declining sales and depressed farm incomes.

Deere's net income was down 50% YoY in the first quarter to 869 million USD. While it will take some time for tariffs to flow through Deere's COGS, I can see tariffs creating something like a 4% hit to its margins, which, I believe, will make it difficult for the company to hit its full-year profitability target.

Financial Services net income totalled 230 million USD in Q1, although was boosted by the sale of Banco John Deere. Underlying Financial Services net income declined due to higher provisions for credit losses. Deere expects 750 million UDS Financial Services net income in FY25, with lower provision for credit losses somewhat offset by less favorable financing spreads. I question whether the expectation of lower credit losses is realistic in the current environment, though.

Deere's job openings are certainly supportive of a rapid rebound in production and sales. While I believe a meaningful improvement from Q1 is likely, I still think the current macro environment will make it difficult for Deere to meet current expectations.

Conclusion

Given that farm income is likely to remain under pressure in the near term, a rebound in Deere's sales is largely dependent on government support for farmers. While some level of support is likely, it will probably be insufficient to fully offset lower commodity prices and tariff headwinds. Even if farm income were to rise, I question whether this would lead to increased equipment sales, as illustrated by current weak dairy and livestock equipment sales, despite strong fundamentals in the segment.

Analysts are projecting a return to YoY growth in the fourth quarter of FY25. Revenue and margins are expected to continue improving from there, although aren't expected to return to the highs of 2023 anytime soon.

The forward path of Deere's share price is dependent on whether current sales represent a cycle trough or a normalization after an unsustainable post-COVID surge. I continue to believe that Deere's sales and margins are normalizing and that the company's revenue multiple will return towards pre-COVID levels as this situation becomes accepted. Even a multiple of 3.5x FY26 sales would see Deere's share price decline to around 300 USD per share.

data.title: ETHE: Is Ethereum Sending A Signal?
data.description:
April 4th brings another session of turmoil and panic in global markets. Equities, commodities, and precious metals are all experiencing another day of deep, across-the-board losses. Of all markets, it is cryptocurrencies that are actually holding up well, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) both green during April 4th trading as of article submission. Frankly, this is a somewhat perplexing development, but I'll get into why I believe there is perhaps a signal in the apparent 'crypto immunity' from global tariff concerns. In this Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:ETHE) article, we'll get into ETH's correlation with tech stocks, holder data, and recent capital flows.

Digital Asset Tariff Immunity?

Let's start with the obvious; cryptocurrencies and digital assets don't actually exist. Shocker, I know. But it happens to be true. Thus, in a world with trade-war escalation, assets that are not bound by physics or geographic borders have perhaps understandably shown little regard for tariff concerns since 'Liberation Day.'

The chart above shows BTC and ETH against proxies for the Nasdaq and S&P 500 as well as Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and Crude Oil (CL1:COM). Through 2pm on April 4th BTC and ETH are holding up while virtually every other major asset or index is down by at least 3%. Digital assets theoretically being immune from jurisdiction risk regarding tariffs is really only one part of this, however. What I still find very perplexing about the action for BTC and ETH is that during stock market panics like the one we're seeing in real time, we often see everything down together as market participants raise liquidity any way they can.

For instance, it's not out of the realm of possibility that profitable Gold positions could be sold by speculators who are de-risking while equities fall apart. One would think BTC and ETH would be getting dragged down in sympathy with the rest of the market due to possible margin calls. But that is not happening as the week of March 31st draws to a close.

It's hard to truly express how bizarre this is because these assets are typically tightly correlated with US equities, particularly tech stocks like the Magnificent Seven. Between mid-March and today, only Tesla (TSLA) has seen correlation with ETH dip below 65%. Most of the stocks shown in the chart above had correlations with ETH in excess of 80%.

Could it truly be that tariffs don't impact cryptos? I'm actually much less sold on that possibility; especially for Bitcoin, which has a US mining footprint that requires machines produced overseas for hashing. This is not a concern for ETH, since Ethereum is a Proof-of-Stake network. Perhaps more likely? Down nearly 60% since mid-December, ETH has already sold off so bad that it's possible that there is simply nobody left who is willing to sell - even raising liquidity could alleviate problems elsewhere.

At 53% of holders currently 'out of the money,' this level of token holders hanging onto losing positions hasn't been seen in ETH in about 5 years. So what do we do with this information? I've been more cautious about Ethereum in recent months on what I view to be a weakening fundamental story, given the network's lack of fees and scaling through secondary layers that don't require nearly as much ETH for gas payment. Given that, it's hard for me to say 'buy ETH' based on any fundamental improvement in the market. But there are other considerations to keep in mind.

Seasonality and Capital Flows

I think we have to take some of the seasonality information with a grain of salt because it flat out did not work in January, February, or March. That said, April and May have historically been the best two months of the year for ETH based on mean change. That figure is 23% for April (2025 is included here) and 31% for May. For me, the bigger thing to pay attention to is whether capital is flowing in or out of ETH-based financial products.

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin -$826 $1,519 $114,475
Ethereum -$370 $316 $9,565
Multi-asset -$206 -$137 $6,366
Solana $27 $86 $1,194
XRP $15 $168 $957

Source: CoinShares, as of March 28th

We know that capital exited ETH products during the last full month of March. However, year to date flow for ETH was still positive at $316 million at the end of the month.

Even though we've seen a little under $60 million come out of Ethereum ETFs so far through three full days in April, the majority of that outflow is from a $31 million single day decline in ETHE on April 2nd. On that same day, an additional $20.2 million came out of the iShares Ethereum ETF (ETHA). The problem ultimately is that ETHE's outflow on the 2nd isn't indicative of Grayscale 'fee flight' continuation:

In fact, ETH ETF share of on-chain supply still favors ETHE which commands over a third of the total market at 38.3%. Importantly, ETHE holding firm in supply share for most of 2025 is probably more indicative of a lack of demand for other funds. For instance, it took a little over 4 months for the iShares product to become the largest ETF holder of Bitcoin in the US spot ETF market. We're 8 months into ETH ETFs in the US market, and ETHE still has a 43% supply edge over ETHA. I read this primarily as a lack of investment demand for Ethereum more broadly.

Closing Takeaways

To answer the question from the headline from the title, I do think ETH is sending a signal. On a day filled with motivated sellers, ETH holders were not among them. Ultimately though, I see more than just one signal in the data. While it's quite encouraging that ETH didn't fall apart with the rest of the market on April 4th, it's perhaps more of an indication that ETH isn't actually as far along with traditional investors as digital asset holders might have hoped. Perhaps the reason ETH isn't getting slammed with the rest of the market is because there just aren't enough people holding it to begin with?

Bottom pickers may find the $1,800 per coin level to be an interesting enough area to make a contrarian trade attempt. I'll re-share something I said in March about how I still currently view ETH technicals:

I see $2,100 per coin serving as support on weekly closes going back to late 2023. That level was breached on a close the week of March 3rd, failed a back test the week of March 10th, and is currently still under that key level as of article submission on March 20th. I, personally, would not entertain taking a stab at that trade from the long side until we see the coin take back $2,100 on a weekly close.

The lack of demand for products like ETHA and similarly lower-priced spot ETFs might remain a headwind of Ethereum bulls should holders keep selling out of ETHE positions. In spite of real problems with ETH's network activity, you could still talk me into holding spot ETH ETFs, but ETHE isn't one of them.

data.title: WTI Oil Technical Outlook: Bears Are Gaining Strength For A Major Bearish Breakdown
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Since our last publication, the price actions of West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) have staged the expected corrective rebound sequence and rallied by 7.2% to print an intraday high of US$72.48/barrel on 2 April (a whisker below the US$73.50 medium-term resistance highlighted in our report) before the announcement of “US Liberation Day” reciprocal trade tariffs.

The West Texas Oil CFD has shaped a significant bearish reversal ex-post “US Liberation Day”, and plummeted by 6% on 3 April, its worst daily loss since early September 2022.

Headwinds from weak demand and excess supply

Fig 1: EIA US crude oil inventories excluding SPR (y/y change) with WTI crude oil futures as of 28 Mar 2025 (Source: MacroMicro)

WTI crude has a negative double whammy hit caused by demand and supply factors. Firstly, the rising probability of a stagflation environment in the US has triggered a global recession alarm, in turn, weakening demand for oil as business activities slow down.

Secondly, OPEC+ producers have agreed to boost the cartel's oil output by 411,000 barrels per day next month, coupled with a recent lesser magnitude of drawdown of US crude oil inventories seen in the past three weeks that may add another layer of downside pressure in oil prices.

The growth of US crude oil inventories excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) on a year-on-year basis has an indirect correlation with the movement of WTI crude oil, as build-up in oil inventories puts downside pressure on oil prices.

Since 13 December 2024, the drawn down of US crude oil inventories (excluding SPR) has slowed down from -5.11% y/y to -2.58% y/y as of 28 March 2025 based on data from the US Energy Information Administration (EIA) (see Fig 1).

US65.40 key support is looking vulnerable

Fig 2: West Texas Oil CFD medium-term trend as of 4 Apr 2025 (Source: TradingView)

Current intraday price actions of the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) have recorded a further drop of 3.2% on Friday, 4 April, and breached below the major “Descending Triangle” range support of US$65.40 at this time of writing (see Fig 2).

In addition, the daily MACD trend indicator has just flashed out a bearish crossover condition, and inched below its centreline, which increases the probability of a major bearish breakdown below the US$65.40 major range support.

A daily close below US$65.40 may trigger the start of a medium-term bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next medium-term support zone of US$$60.20/58.80.

However, a clearance above the US$72.50 medium-term pivotal resistance (also the key 200-day moving average) invalidates the bearish scenario to kickstart another round of choppy corrective rebound phase for the next medium-term resistances to come in at US$76.00 and US$80.30

Original Post

data.title: SPY: What Market Tops Look And Feel Like, Is It Different This Time?
data.description:
"If it be now, 'tis not to come. If it be not to come it will be now. If it be not now, yet it will come -the readiness is all." Shakespeare. Hamlet and Horatio were speaking of death, perhaps with a side glance at market crashes.

The Great Crash of 1929 was the model for all crashes that followed. It marked the end of a stupendous economic boom and led to a prolonged period of pain. It's not that there hadn't been speculative fevers before, including a few which had consequences for a large part of a nation's population. Some of the major ones are detailed in Charles McKay's Extraordinary Popular Delusions And The Madness Of Crowds (1841). It's a fun read, but it's not so much the story of economic events as of schemes and manias that often reveal tragicomic aspects of human behavior and psychology.

The first full-fledged treatment of a stock market crash occurring as the end of a broad economic boom was the semi-scholarly but popular The Great Crash, 1929, by John Kenneth "Ken" Galbraith. Galbraith was a favorite professor I got to know while taking his course on the Modern Industrial State in 1967. He was amused by the fact that I had read his 1929 book at age 12 just a year after its publication. I have read it again every few years and find that it always sharpens my thinking about crashes. I will cite some of his facts and views in this article.

Galbraith held no brief for those who thought that the 1929 Crash was not a major cause of the 1930s Depression. He saw the Crash as a major force damaging the economy and making almost everyone poorer. As for the underlying cause, he saw it as stemming from the belief in America at that time that it was easy to get rich quickly with little effort. I was not born until 15 years after the 29 Crash, but I have been around and involved in the markets for every subsequent boom/bust cycle. The details differ but the basic events are very similar. Here's a market chart representing the emotions of investors as the market goes through a full cycle. The chart is a little gaudy for my taste, but matches in detail the most common pattern of the majority of crashes as well as stock market behavior before and after. Note the failed rally which follows the initial crash. I wrote in a recent article that if an attempt to rally fails, look out below!

Many booms have a single stock which defines the era. Today it is clearly Nvidia (NVDA). The market leader during the 1920s boom was Radio Corporation of America. The radio was the major new technology of the of the 1920s, but like the present company Apple (AAPL) it was both a tech stock and a consumer stock providing a new way to advertise goods and services. Radio Corporation rose by leaps and bounds in the late 20s, topping out at a high of $568 per share in September 1929, only to lead the market on the downside following the Crash by falling about 98% to a 1932 low of $15.

Were the 1920's a "New Era"? Not exactly. There were no real innovations beyond the radio, and established stocks like General Motors were also market leaders. The United States had emerged victorious from WWI and taken its place among the leading nations. The rich got richer, and as Galbraith put it, "the poor got less poor." It was as if someone had managed to turn up the heat in everything. In 1927, Babe Ruth hit a record 60 home runs, becoming such a symbol of American power that Japanese banzai charges in WWII were accompanied by shouts specifically disparaging Ruth. Charles Lindbergh became the first to fly across the Atlantic. Flappers turned up the heat on the dance floor, attired in revealing knee length dresses that suggested a looser code of sexual conduct. They danced fast. Fast dancing has a surprising correlation to good economic times.

The markets seemed to be on fire. Then the Crash happened.

Detailed Data That Preceded The 1929 Top

Certain specific pieces of data draw a picture of a solidly growing economy through the middle of 1929, as it was pretty much onward and upward for everything having to do with the economy and the stock market. Some of the data below is from Galbraith and some is my own.

  • The Florida land boom of 1924-26 prefigured the sequence of events that was to follow. Its major figure was Charles Ponzi, whose name is immortalized in the term "Ponzi scheme." Ponzi sold Florida lots unseen and created a sort of multi-level marketing in which higher prices led to even higher prices until it ran out of buyers and the whole edifice collapsed.
  • As late as January 1, 1926, stock P/E ratios, as measured by Standard and Poor's, were just over 10, near the average for that era. There are many estimates for P/E ratios in 1929 but by the same source used for 1926, they were just over 20, a double. On September 3, 1929, however, they may have been about 30. Except for the 1926 number, these P/Es resemble the present.
  • The European national banks persuaded the Fed to cut rates from 4% to 3.5% in 1927. This easier money was the start of rampant speculation. Investors sold bonds and bought stocks. Daily moves in individual stocks and the market as a whole became much more volatile in 1928.
  • The economy had begun to slow in June, 1929, four months before the crash, with declines in steel and factory output
  • The least savvy investors purchased stocks on margin of up to 50% of market value. They paid 10% for the money. Margin calls by brokers caused the Crash to accelerate.
  • Only about 4% of the population owned stocks versus 60% today.
  • Highly leveraged investment trusts, which mostly sold at a premium to market value, sold heavily as the market fell.
  • Prominent industrialists and bankers reassured investors that all was well and continued to do so through the beginnings of the crash. Irving Fisher, the most prominent economist of the era, famously told the public that "stock prices have reached what looks like a permanently high plateau."
  • The Rockefeller family bought stocks and made their buying public in hopes of stemming the tide. Nothing availed
  • Financier Bernard Baruch, advisor to two Presidents, had gotten entirely out of the market very early. Two brothers in law, Alfred Lee Loomis and Landon Thorne, sold their stocks early and bought Treasury bonds. They not only received the 4.5% yield on bonds but profited from the fact that the 80% deflation of the 1930s made every dollar purchase five times as much as it had in the 1920s.
  • The "shoe shine boy" anecdote encapsulates the moment. Joe Kennedy, father of President John Kennedy and one of the richest men in America, sold his entire position in stocks when a shoe shine boy offered him a list of stocks which should perform well. The story may or may not be an exaggeration, but bad things are on the way when shoe shine boys offer investment advice to multi millionaires.
  • In the 1930s, the Flappers disappeared. Women wore long dresses in the 1930s, and dancing became slow and soothing.
  • The Smoot-Hawley tariffs, about 20% across the board, was close to the average Trump tariff estimate. Smoot-Hawley didn't cause the Crash because the bill was enacted in 1930, but most analysts believe they deepened the Depression that followed.

The Public Mood Preceding Crashes

The above chart looks a great deal like the 1929 chart. The chart above the title is the actual chart of the moderate crash of 2020 as the public gradually became aware of the pandemic and possible economic shut down on the way. The topping process was slight, as the market fell from an all-time high to a crash bottom within a few weeks.

In 2020, it took investors only a couple of weeks to go from being confident to being scared to death of what the pandemic would bring. The market fell about 37%. With the help of lower rates and and a huge increase in Federal spending, the market quickly recovered. The loose fiscal policy overstayed the need for it and created the high inflation beginning in 2022.

The 1987 Crash, which had a chart much like 1929, also managed to come back within a few months and make a new all-time high within a year. Many came to believe that the 1987 crash was brought about by "portfolio insurance," which compelled institutional selling as the market fell to certain established levels.

The blue chip growth stocks of the early 1970s were seen by the markets as so reliable that they came to be called one-decision stocks (buy and never sell) and referred to as the Nifty Fifty. You can look up a list of them by googling Nifty Fifty. Their price/earnings ratios often rose to 50 or more, until the severe bear market of 1973-74 dragged them down along with the less "nifty" parts of the market.

High and increasing inflation added to make the mood of the 1970s grim and dark. Legend has it that President Carter called it a "malaise" (he never actually used that word) in a very unpopular Presidential address. I never bit on the idea that there were one-decision stocks, but bought a few of the Nifty Fifty in the early 1980s when they had fallen to single digit P/E ratios. Hey, do any of you kids out there believe that there was a time when the whole market had a P/E ratio well under 10?

My great experience of what a boom/bust/crash sequence feels like was in the couple of years leading up to the dot-com crash of 2000. Over a couple of years, I noticed that the weight room at the indoor club where I taught tennis was getting so busy that it was necessary to get in line for the workout machines. Even free weights had to be grabbed quickly, but with great care not to get bopped on the head. Everybody was busy talking about how much money they were making in tech stocks, and the TV on the wall had a ticker tape and stock interviews instead of sports events. These were middle-aged upper middle class people who were good at their jobs but knew next to nothing about the market. I tried to reason with some of the dot-com enthusiasts who were gym friends, but they weren't having any of it. Valuations were meaningless. The dot-com stocks were good at any price. The Internet was the future. They thought I was just a sour grapes idiot who had failed to get in early.

Cocktail parties were even worse. All people wanted to talk about was stocks. All I could say was that I owned a few small caps plus two emerging market CEFs, India and Brazil, plus some Treasury bonds. The cocktail party favorite was Nokia (NOK). It was so annoying to hear people talk about it that I did a little analysis and saw a number of business problems on the way.

Nokia had risen from $4.98 in 1998 to $68 in 2000, only to fall and keep falling until it sold for $1.05 (in 2012). By 2003, it began to be easier to get my favorite weight machines and use the benches and free weights. The workout room was quiet. No one said anything about stocks. No one stood up to announce how much they had lost. The more boastful people had disappeared one by one. There were fewer cocktail parties. People no longer seized upon any excuse requiring me to waste time and wear a necktie.

It was more of the same during the real estate/Mortgage Backed Securities crisis between 2007 and 2009. The events between 2007 and 2009 are probably recent enough that many investors remember them. It was another bubble made up of instant experts but more lower middle to middle class including some who were out of work and borrowing to speculate. More than any other crash event, it resembles the Florida boom and bust crash of 1925-1926, with its own enabling Ponzis who led so many of the badly informed to believe that they could easily flip houses at a big profit. It also involved corporate CEOs more than willing to look the other way as tranches of Mortgage Backed Securities ("MBS") were put together on the premise that a few a thousand MBS, none of which could be repaid, were somehow safer than one individual MBS that couldn't be repaid.

Quite a few miscreants came to the surface with the exposure of shenanigans with Enron, one of whom I knew well for his public integrity and philosophy of giving back to the less fortunate. One day, I saw him on TV trying to explain away some very questionable practices at Enron. He was forced to resign from the Harvard board.

It was hard not to succumb to a bit of schadenfreude when many of the former holier-than-thou set ended up ruined or in jail. It makes you wonder if McKay's book on the Madness of Crowds is the more fundamental textbook.

What's Different This Time?

Mark Twain, who was often all too right, is credited with saying the future doesn't repeat the past, but it rhymes. Note that I didn't say this time is fundamentally different. It rhymes sort of like free verse poetry. There is always, for instance, overvaluation of stocks (or real estate), and this time is no exception. The thing to do is to look at a few anomalies and see what close inspection suggests as the most likely future course. I have noticed a bit of debate as to whether this is merely a correction or a crash followed by a recession with real consequences. This is probably the wrong way to frame the question. The market is radically overvalued, and will give us no peace or possibility of long-term gain until that excess valuation is wrung out of the system. It's not just the tariffs, although they certainly don't help and perhaps provided the trigger for what was inevitable anyway. So let's look at a few areas where today's market is a little different from past crises.

Understanding that a few crash indicators are absent in the current market or different enough that a large correction process may have certain differences which may have an impact on the way the decline unfolds. Mind me, such differences won't mean that is a good idea to run out and buy stocks. To the contrary, I'm pretty sure that a long term adjustment of market valuation is going to take place in a way that will be unpleasant to miserable over the next ten years. What I'm less sure of is whether there will be a major crash event, and if so, whether it is likely to take place right now or a bit later. A close look at a few missing elements of a boom/bust/crash environment may help understand what shape a valuation adjustment will likely take. The chart below helps set the stage for a couple of anomalies starting with the question as to whether the market is too rational for a crash to be on the way or underway.

  • What the above chart shows is that since the market began to decline sharply there is a certain logic within the decline. The conservative and value-oriented Dow (DJI) has moved to the lead. Since January 1, the Dow has opened up an increasing lead over both the NASDAQ (QQQ), the major growth index, and the SPDR S&P 500 Index Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY), which has a balance of value (risk-off) and growth (risk-on). As the market declined there was a clear tug of war between value and growth, with value, on balance, winning. It's no surprise that the SPY 500, which owns both value and growth, is somewhere in the middle.
  • Is the market too rational for a Crash? Market crashes are not driven by rationality but by cries of Help! Get me out! What the above chart says is that the stodgy old Dow is doing better than either the QQQ or SPY. The simple explanation is that the Dow is both more value-oriented and less overpriced. It should go down to a degree with the market but hold up better. I published this article, entitled "The Dow Likely Beats The S&P 500 Over The Next Several Years," on May 1, 2024, almost a year ago. It didn't surprise me that it got just 2788 readers, close to the lowest I have ever gotten on any article over 12 years. I would probably get more readers on an article about how to bake a potato or make a ham sandwich, and I expected as much, but wrote it anyway because the argument behind actually seemed important.
  • You could can also see from the above chart that the decline appeared initially mainly a correction in overpriced growth stocks, especially the Mag 7. Extending the argument with further detail, note that their prices reflect perfect outcomes far into the future and it was inevitable that sooner or later investors would begin to question whether AI was really going to add enough to the growth and profits of these stocks to warrant such high prices. About a week ago it became less likely that the rest of the market was OK. As time passed it became increasingly likely that the Mag 7 and other growth stocks were just getting hit the hardest in an overall market move to reduce valuations of everything.
  • But where's the speculative fever? This is the weird one. Peak speculative fever was actually a couple of years ago. Ark Innovation ETF (ARKK), a good proxy for speculative lunacy, actually topped out in February of 2022 and I published this article on March 11, 2022, discussing the crash of ARKK and noting that 22 of 35 stocks in its portfolio had negative cash flow. Most of the buyers of ARKK bought then as the ETF was peaking which tells you about the innocence of those buyers. The mini crash along with the rest of the market starting in February of this year is just an echo of the more serious declines of more serious stocks.
  • Where are the shoeshine boys? The club where I work out has not been crowded for several years. The few regulars are now much younger, including a few dedicated high school kids along with a handful of old guys like me. Nobody talks about the markets and the six TVs in the cycle rooms often don't have a single TV with a ticker tape. Meanwhile, the 30ish kids I teach tennis with show me their portfolios, occasionally nod their heads to my cautionary advice, and then keep doing what they are doing, many of their stocks coming from ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK).
  • Where's the complacency? I have raised the subject of market risk with a few long-time friends, sometimes when they are doing bike workouts, and the uniform answer is that everybody knows that the market always goes up in the long run. It does, but there have been 10 to 20 year periods when it didn't. Meanwhile, they are comfortable just using the S&P 500 in their IRA accounts despite the fact that the cap weighted SPY or VOO (and other replications of the 500) recently had 35% of the index in its 10 largest stocks. They feel safe enough not to worry about things like excessive concentration or sky high valuation of the market as a whole. When I advise them to at least think about it, their eyes glaze over. They just don't want to hear it. The S&P 500 is probably the most guaranteed risky part of the market (unless you own QQQ or the lot) and it's a mistake to not at least be aware of the risks.
  • There's an odd contradiction between bullish actions and bearish opinions. Polls of small investors show them as very cautious and bearish, but their actions don't reflect bearishness. Yet. Maybe tomorrow. What's that about?

My Personal Strategy

My overall strategy is to remain invested but in value stocks which have more growth than analysts recognize and have a strong defense. This is not very different than the structure of my portfolio when I think the market is likely to coast right along. It has in fact narrowly beaten the market over the years, although that's not a goal. Last year, I trimmed my overly large Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) position just because it was simply too large. Warren Buffett himself did the equivalent by letting his buybacks dwindle to zero by the middle of last year. As CEO, he is certainly not going to sell, although Ajit Jain did, but not buying back shares is the moral equivalent of selling, something he can't do but I can. He showed his hand by dumping a large part of his Apple (AAPL) position, taking the heat for it from critics who felt he now had way too much cash. So much for that criticism. Never forget: Buffett has never been wrong about major market turns.

(One caution: by defensive stocks, I emphatically do not mean consumer staples or utilities which are as overvalued for what they are as any part of the market. They are just an easy way for publications to give advice that seems good until you think about it.)

My second major move has been to buy Treasury Bonds and other very safe CDs up to the maximum $250K covered by the FDIC. I started doing this in October/November of 2023 and wrote about it here. The article is a primer on bonds which many investors have forgotten about during the Fed's suppression of rates, and if you don't know the basics about bonds, I encourage you to read it two or three times. I have continued to extend maturities and lucked out just yesterday when I bought a very large 10-year Treasury Bond just hours before rates fell from 4.04% to 3.94%. The decision to buy bonds gets harder every day as rates fall in the panic over the current market decline. Your decision, of course. I estimate my overall rate in bonds and CDs is around 4.7%.

My primary observation in this article is that many investors have a mistaken sense of security. The above chart suggests that the S&P 500 Index Funds and ETFs, especially leveraged ETS, are anything but safe. They are just a sort of mid point between wildly overpriced growth stocks and value stocks with prices that have been pulled up along with the prices of growth stocks. My main whipping boy for this has been Walmart (WMT), a perfectly fine company which was selling at a P/E ration of 36 at the top before the decline since early March took it down to 34. It has a minuscule dividend and very little growth. It would need to go down 40% to be anywhere close to fair value. Ask yourself what it would take to make it go up 40% to a P/E of 48?

I sit patiently and calculate every day whether I should change anything in my approach. The market is unlikely to do well over several years. I'm pretty sure of that as you readers know if you have read my three most recent articles. Valuations are very likely to come down. This can happen quickly with a Crash or more slowly and painfully with a long term drag on prices. I truly have no idea which. I suspect that answer may lie hidden in the anomalies above but I haven't quite worked it out. Today's investors are more mature and better informed than more speculative investors have been in boom/bust/crash situations in the past.

Bear in mind that sneaky statistic that 60% of the population are now stock investors versus 4% in 1929. We can't really know how that much that larger number impacts market risk. There's always the risk that a little knowledge can be a dangerous thing - like the tag line that stocks always go up. Yes, probably, but maybe not in my lifetime (age 80). What will finally cause the buy-the-dippers to throw in the towel and how rapidly will the decline unfold? Your guess is as good as mine.

That's about the sum of what I know. Good luck and good investing. Write comments and I will be happy to reply.

data.title: EOS: Price Drop Creates An Attractive Opportunity For Accumulation
data.description:
Overview

Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (NYSE:EOS) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of common equities. The fund also deploys an option strategy, which helps generate additional income for shareholders. I previously covered EOS at the end of 2024 and issued a buy rating due to its ability to outperform during a market slowdown. Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report, so I wanted to revisit EOS to provide some updated analysis of the fund's performance regarding current market conditions.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that EOS's price has increased by about 3.15%, even after the recent decline due to ongoing market sensitivity to President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 16% over the same period. One of the main appeals of EOS is the generous starting dividend yield of about 8.5%. This makes EOS a great option for retired investors looking to extract income from a portfolio of underlying equities.

Following recent market declines, EOS's price has experienced a sizable decline. I believe this presents an attractive opportunity for accumulation for investors planning to hold for the long term. The recent declines have now caused EOS to trade at a small discount to NAV valuation. The fund maintains exposure to the highest quality large-cap companies in the world, so when conditions shift to a more positive outlook, EOS shareholders will directly benefit. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's underlying strategy and investment methodology.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, total net assets amount to $1.2B and are spread across 48 different holdings. The fund primarily focuses on large-cap holdings that management believes have the ability to provide above-average growth according to earnings trajectory, sales growth, and balance sheet strength. As a result, EOS is most heavily allocated towards the Information Technology sector, making up 47.56% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary investments, making up 14.17% and 13.54% of assets respectively.

Since the portfolio primarily leans towards the Technology sector, many of the top holdings come as no surprise. NVIDIA (NVDA) is currently the largest individual position at 11.97% of assets. This is followed by a 9.15% weight in Microsoft (MSFT) and an 8.71% weight in Amazon (AMZN). Many of the top holdings have continued to decline on a YTD basis in response to ongoing tariff tensions. The current makeup of holdings within EOS will likely lead to a higher price volatility for the remainder of 2025.

On a more positive note, many of the underlying holdings within EOS are aligned to benefit from the growth of the AI market in some aspect. For instance, NVDA is leading the charge with their GPUs, Alphabet (GOOG) is working on establishing new implementations of their AI tools like Gemini, and Meta Platforms (META) continues to make strides with their wearable technology that has built-in AI software, such as their Ray-Ban collaboration. If you are bullish on the growth of AI, EOS is a great fund to be in since shareholders are directly aligned to benefit.

To offset market volatility, EOS also implements an option writing strategy against portions of its underlying holdings. The goal here is to harness market volatility as a way to generate consistent premium income. EOS utilizes OTM (out-of-the-money) options, which still allows for some price appreciation to be captured during bull markets. The downside to an option strategy is that the upside potential is effectively capped at whatever strike price is selected on written options. This is exactly why we see EOS underperforming traditional ETFs like Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM) from a price change perspective. Over the last approximate four-year period, QQQM has increased by over 54% while EOS only increased by a bit over 5%.

To demonstrate how this option strategy works, EOS may hold shares of an asset priced at $100 per share. EOS will write an option against this asset and may select a strike price of $105 per share. If the underlying asset has a strong quarterly earnings report and the price appreciates up to $115 per share, EOS will only capture the upside movement limited to the strike price of $105 per share. EOS only partially captures this upside movement in exchange for the option premiums collected. Therefore, I will always expect EOS to underperform during strong bull markets.

Updated Valuation

Following the recent price decline, EOS's price now trades at a very slight discount to NAV. Entry while shares trade at a discount to NAV can be a great long-term opportunity because this signals that there is a disconnect between the price action and the actual value of the underlying net assets. For reference, the price has traded at an average discount to NAV of 1.8% over the last three-year period. Looking back between 2018 and 2023, we can see that the price frequently traded at a slight premium to NAV, indicating that EOS's NAV can remain relatively stable under less stressful market conditions. Once conditions shift to a more positive sentiment, I believe that EOS can start to trade back into the premium territory.

Since the time of my last coverage, EOS has released an updated annual report for the entire year of 2024. The year delivered a total income from operations of $5.31 per share. Net investment income saw a loss of $0.11 per share, but this was offset by $5.42 per share worth of net realized gains from sale of underlying positions. Since the fund is primarily concentrated to Technology stocks, net investment income has consistently reported at a loss. Tech companies aren't typically known to produce consistent dividends, since they choose to reinvest their funds back into the business.

Therefore, most of the growth has come from net realized gains within. This fund has done a great job at capturing gains during bullish market movements and since markets tend to rise more than they fall, EOS has experienced healthy NAV growth in recent years. The NAV has steadily recovered from its low of 2022. For instance, here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $21.20
  • 2021: $23.72
  • 2022: $15.75
  • 2023: $19.71
  • 2024: $23.30

The fund's ability to efficiently capture growth during positive market years gives me the confidence that the fund will ultimately recover from any headwinds that it faces. Therefore, I believe that accumulation during the current market weakness is certainly an attractive opportunity for long-term investors.

Risk Profile

The main risk here would be that EOS is likely to continue underperforming traditional index-tracking ETFs. For instance, EOS has underperformed both the S&P 500 (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) in total return over the last decade, despite having a higher dividend yield. The inclusion of an option strategy will always suffer from the capped upside potential. Therefore, investors seeking to maximize the total return potential within their portfolio are better off sticking with more traditional ETFs that do not put an emphasis on income generation.

EOS's smaller number of holdings has also made the fund a bit more volatile than traditional ETFs. For instance, EOS has a total loss of about 9% on a YTD basis, which is much greater than QQQ's loss of 6.7% and SPY's loss of 3.4% over the same time frame. This can be attributed to the combination of a more focused batch of holdings and the fact that EOS will continue to pay out distributions, even if it hasn't actually earned enough to support them. Paying out distributions without the actual earnings to support them will inevitably lead to fast NAV and price deterioration.

However, the fund can act as a middle ground between the S&P 500 and Nasdaq. For instance, markets declined throughout 2022 due to a Fed rate shock. The Fed aggressively hiked interest rates throughout 2022 in response to record high inflation rates. This caused indexes to decline, with SPY had a total loss of more than 18% while QQQ had a loss of more than 32%. However, the higher distributions received from EOS helped offset total returns a bit, leading to a total loss of only 26.5%, compared to QQQ's 32.5% loss.

Goldman Sachs released a report that estimated the S&P 500's average annual growth to be 3% over the next decade. This is a far way off from the +10% average growth investors have experienced over the last decade. According to the report, there is a risk that the growth of tech stocks will slow because consistent double-digit growth rates are unlikely to continue. For instance, the report indicates that only 11% of S&P 500 companies have been able to maintain double-digit sales growth for at least ten consecutive years. Perhaps expectations around the projected growth of AI will not materialize as expected. EOS is vulnerable to a slowdown in tech growth since most of the fund is allocated towards tech.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.1523 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.5%. If we take the monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.8276 per share. Referring back to the annual report, EOS has not had an issue supporting this distribution amount in the past. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts per share - only one of the last five years saw negative earnings:

  • 2020: $4.76 per share
  • 2021: $3.89 per share
  • 2022: ($6.38) per share
  • 2023: $5.34 per share
  • 2024: $5.31 per share

The earnings for 2024 were enough to effectively support the current distribution for almost 3 years. The fund's distribution history isn't really a reflection of whether or not the fund can afford to support them. Instead, EOS actively adjusts the distribution amount to reflect the current strength of the market conditions. For instance, the distribution amount was reduced after 2022's weak performance. However, payouts were raised again following 2023's positive earnings performance.

The fund is essentially paying out all of the gains in the form of a distribution. While it may not be the most reliable from an income standpoint, due to the possibility of shifting payouts, it's still a great way to extract gains from a portfolio of diverse equities. However, the most recent section19(a) notice indicates that 56.3% of the distributions YTD have been funded by return of capital. This could indicate that the fund hasn't been able to generate sufficient returns over 2025. As expected, it can be difficult to achieve net realized gains during market declines. Return of capital distributions aren't taxed directly. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced and taxes are deferred until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on EOS due to the healthy NAV growth demonstrated, which reinforces that the fund can successfully capitalize on positive market movements. Despite recent headwinds over the last few months related to tariffs and other external influences, EOS is well-positioned to reward long-term shareholders. The prior year's earnings were enough to sustain the current distribution amount, and the fund's holdings align shareholders to benefit from technology growth over the next decade.

data.title: Neogen Corporation: A Second Assessment, Likely Impacted By New Tariff Policies
data.description:
Today, I am putting Neogen Corporation (NASDAQ:NEOG) in the spotlight for the first time since my initial assessment on the company back in June 2024. The conclusion on that article was to avoid the shares due primarily to valuations. The stock has been more than cut in half since that piece was published here on Seeking Alpha. Two company officers purchased new equity in Neogen in January. Are the shares of this small-cap concern in the "bargain bin" yet? An updated assessment follows below.

Neogen Corporation is headquartered in Lansing, MI and operates two main business segments: Food Safety and Animal Safety. The company is focused on the manufacture and marketing of myriad products and services dedicated to food and animal safety globally. These include various diagnostic tools like the recently launched Neogen Molecular Detection Assay 2 used to detect salmonella. Neogen merged with the 3M Food Safety Division in the summer of 2022 after announcing this transaction in late 2021.

The stock currently trades just below eight bucks a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $1.75 billion. Neogen has a quite unusual fiscal year that begins on May 1st. We are currently in firm's fiscal 2025 year.

Recent Results

The company posted its Q2 numbers on January 10th. Neogen Corporation delivered non-GAAP earnings of 11 cents a share, a penny a share above the consensus. This was flat with the same period a year ago. Adjusted net income came in at $24.4 million, with adjusted EBITDA of $51.4 million during the quarter. Both were slight declines from 2Q2024. The GAAP net loss for the quarter came in at a huge $456.3 million, which was due almost all to a non-cash goodwill impairment.

Revenues rose .7% on a year-over-year basis to $231.3 million, some $3 million north of expectations. Foreign currency fluctuations took 2.5% from sales growth it should be noted. Lower genomics revenue from recent restructuring actions were also cited. Animal Safety revenues were $67 million for the quarter, up seven percent without genomics and currency impacts. A rise of three percent including genomics revenue. Leadership highlighted some of the core accomplishments/trends during the quarter.

Management lowered FY 2025 guidance due to currency impacts and other factors. Projected revenues were taken down from $925 million to $955 million at the end of first quarter to $905 million to $925 million. Expected Adjusted EBITDA was also decreased $20 million.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet

Despite a significant market cap, Neogen Corporation gets very little coverage from Wall Street. Only two analyst firms I can find that have chimed in on the company over the past 12 months via TipRanks. Guggenheim reissued its Buy rating and $15 price target on December 19th and Piper Sandler maintained its Hold rating on January 21st and lowered their price target from $16.50 to $13.00 a share.

Just under 11% of the overall float in the shares are currently held short. The CFO and CEO purchased just under $420,000 of equity collectively in the first half of January. Those are the only insider transactions so far in 2025, however. It should be noted that insiders were net buyers of the stock in 2022, 2023 and 2024. Quite unusual across the U.S. equity space.

Neogen Corporation ended Q2 2025 with approximately $140 million of cash and marketable securities against some $900 million worth of debt on its balance sheet. Free cash flow improved notably sequentially from the first quarter, and debt was decreased some $20 million in the quarter as well. Management has guided the company is targeting $85 million in capital expenditures in FY 2025. This included approximately $55 million related specifically to the integration of the former 3M Food Safety Division. Cap Ex will be much less in the second half of FY 2025 ($30 million) than the first half and should continue to drop as integration is now largely complete.

Conclusion

Neogen Corporation made 45 cents a share on just over $924 million in revenues in FY 2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling slightly to 43 cents a share in FY 2026 as sales fall to $910 million. They project earnings to rebound to 52 cents a share on four percent sales growth in FY 2027.

This leaves the stock trading at around 18 times earnings and 1.9 times revenues. The shares do not pay a dividend. The company has a significant debt load. Even with the big recent decline in the stock, that is not bargain territory given both profits and revenues are expected to slightly decline in FY 2025. I went through the past two quarterly earnings call transcripts and presentations, and it doesn't appear that the company breaks down sales by regions. However, they do appear to get considerable revenues from overseas. They are likely to impacted further by the new tariff policies and the emergence of a potential global trade war.

Therefore, I would not be surprised if management reduced guidance further following its next quarterly update. Bottom line, I still don't find the valuations around NEOG compelling, especially given the deteriorating market environment. I will continue to tag the shares with an avoid rating.

data.title: Gentex Corporation And Its Real Value (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Headquartered in Zeeland, Michigan, Gentex Corporation (NASDAQ:GNTX) is a technology company and long-term supplier of electro-optical products for the global automotive, fire protection and medical industries. Gentex is best known for automotive electronics, but continues to grow its capabilities in vision systems, sensing, AI development, biometrics, home automation, and other smart technologies.

Gentex recently acquired VOXX in an all-cash transaction at a purchase price of $7.50 per share. The acquisition process was completed on April 1, 2025. VOXX International is a leader in the automotive and consumer electronics industry, has built market-leading positions in the in-vehicle entertainment and automotive security.

The hope is that this acquisition will help Gentex to realize the vision of “uniting automotive consumer electronic industries”.

According to Neil Bohem, the Gentex Chief Operations Officer and Chief Technology Officer, Gentex is looking to continue to expand beyond Gentex’s existing business lines, and VOXX will bring expertise in consumer product distribution, retail sales and mass merchandising. Additionally, this merger and acquisition opportunity signify collaborations in applying Gentex’s expertise in engineering and electronics manufacturing to various VOXX lines beyond the automotive industry. Currently, Gentex is also involved in the research and development of nanofiber chemical sensing products.

When considering these current stories about Gentex, we need to determine which news topics will have a long-term and ongoing effect on the company and its share price. While the drop in demand is a worrisome development, the company is adamant that it’s a short-term hinderance. Conversely, GNTX’s manufacturing expansion and growing product line beyond the automotive industry will provide long-term benefits and potential.

This article will focus on the long-term fundamentals of the company, which tend to give us a better picture of the company as a viable investment. I also analyze the value of the company versus the price and help you to determine if GNTX is currently trading at a bargain price. I provide various situations which help estimate the company's future returns. In closing, I will tell you my personal opinion about whether I'm interested in taking a position in this company and why.

Snapshot of the Company

A fast way for me to get an overall understanding of the condition of the business is to use the BTMA Stock Analyzer’s company rating score. GNTX has a good company rating score of 84.5 out of 100. In summary, GNTX appears to have above average fundamentals.

Before jumping to conclusions, we’ll have to look closer into individual categories to see what’s going on.

Fundamentals

Let’s examine the price per share history first. In the chart below, we can see that price per share has been on a fairly steady incline over the past 10 years. Overall, share price average has grown by about 142% over the past 10 years or a Compound Annual Growth Rate of 10.32%.

Earnings

Looking closer at earnings history, we see that earnings have grown overall during the past 10 years. The earnings became more volatile and shakier from 2020 – 2022, but this is understandable since Covid-19 caused many disruptions.

The impacts of EV production, tariffs and local manufacturing cycles also coincided with a longer-term trend of more demand in driver assisted driving option in cars and increased EV production taking over more market share from traditional auto products. Potential risks in the future lie in President’s Trumps imposed tariffs in universal auto products in the automotive industry worldwide. However, consistent and steady earnings in the past make it easier to accurately estimate the future growth and value of the company while factoring in potential risks in policies changes. So, in this regard, Gentex is a good example of a stock to more accurately estimate future growth or current value.

Since earnings and price per share don’t always give the whole picture, it’s good to look at other factors like the gross margins, return on equity, and return on invested capital.

Return on Equity

The return on equity has ranged between 15.5% and 19.5% over the past 5 years. Despite the disruptions of Covid-19, the company did a reasonably good job at maintaining its Return on Equity. For return on equity (ROE), I look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, GNTX meets my requirements.

Let’s compare the ROE of this company to its industry. The average ROE of Auto Parts companies is 7.09%.

Therefore, GNTX’s 5-year average of 17.48% is well above the industry average.

Return on Invested Capital

The return on invested capital has remained relatively stable over the past five years with only slight dips in 2022 and 2024. The dip in 2022 is likely due to chip storage issues and shipping delays in the auto industry. High Interest rates in 2024 have likely resulted in harder financing and hence slowing revenue growth.

The 5-year average ROIC is above expectations at around 17.48%. For return on invested capital (ROIC), I also look for a 5-year average of 16% or more. So, GNTX passes this test as well.

Gross Margin Percent

The gross margin percent (GMP) has slightly decreased over the last five years. Even with the declines, the overall gross margin percent remains at decent levels. Five-year GMP average is around 34%.

I typically look for companies with gross margin percent consistently above 30%. So, GNTX has proven that it has the ability to maintain acceptable margins over a long period.

Financial Stability

Looking at other fundamentals involving the balance sheet, we can see that the debt-to-equity is less than 1. This is a positive indicator, telling us that the company owns more than it owes.

GNTX’s Current Ratio of 4.11 is satisfactory, indicating it has an adequate ability to use its assets to pay its short-term debt.

Ideally, we’d want to see a Current Ratio of more than 1, so GNTX exceeds this amount.

According to the balance sheet, the company appears to be in good financial health. In the long term, the company has more than enough assets to cover its debts. In the short-term the company is generating enough cash flow, to fulfill its obligations.

GNTX pays a dividend on a quarterly basis, its annual dividend is currently $0.48.

This analysis wouldn’t be complete without considering the value of the company vs. share price.

Value Vs. Price

The company’s Price-Earnings Ratio of 13.3 indicates that GNTX might be selling at a high price when comparing GNTX’s P/E Ratio to a long-term market average P/E Ratio of 15.

The 10-year and 5-year average P/E Ratio of GNTX has typically been 17.6 and 19.2, respectively. This indicates that GNTX could be currently trading at a low price when comparing to its average historical P/E Ratio range.

The Estimated Value of the Stock is $28.54, versus the current stock price of $22.08. This indicates that Gentex is currently selling at a bargain price.

For more detailed valuation purposes, I will be using a conservative EPS of 1.76. I’ve used various past averages of growth rates and P/E Ratios to calculate different scenarios of valuation ranges from low to average values. The valuations compare growth rates of EPS, Book Value, and Total Equity.

In the table below, you can see the different scenarios, and in the chart, you will see vertical valuation lines that correspond to the table valuation ranges. The dots on the lines represent the current stock price. If the dot is towards the bottom of the valuation range, this would indicate that the stock is undervalued. If the dot is near the top of the valuation line, this would show an overvalued stock.

This analysis shows an average valuation of around $28.53 per share versus its current price of about $22, this would indicate that Gentex Corp. stock is undervalued.

Summarizing the Fundamentals

According to the data shown above, Gentex Corporation is financially healthy in a long-term sense in having enough equity as compared with debt, and in the short-term because the current ratio indicates that it has plenty of cash to cover current liabilities.

This company has an impressive earnings record, which shows consistent and improving earnings over a 10-year period.

Other fundamentals are at sufficient levels, including ROE, ROIC, and Gross Margins. The only downside of the fundamentals is that ROE, ROIC, and Gross Margins have experienced slight decreases in 2022 and 2024. One reason could be a result of higher interest rates, supply chain disruptions in the automotive industry, and cutting into margins. President Trump has just imposed a 10% tariff on all imports; those policies must be factored into the future outlook and fundamentals of Gentex.

Another consideration is that Gentex has remained resilient and has overcome may supply chain issues over the past 5 years. Gentex has in fact experienced a steady incline in business earnings during Covid-19. Gentex is operating in a niche industry where it is one of the few suppliers providing auto mirror systems with built-in electronics.

The overall progression in auto productions is moving more towards driver-assisted technology, which should make Gentex’s technology more resistant to reduced vehicle sales in the event of decreased car productions after President Trump’s imposed tariff policies.

In terms of valuation, my analysis shows that the stock is undervalued.

GNTX Vs. The S&P 500

Now, let’s see how GNTX compares versus the US stock market benchmark S&P 500 over the past 10 years. From the chart below, we can see that GNTX has slightly outperformed the general market for most of the time from 2013 to 2024. The most recent year has shown that GNTX has underperformed and this could indicate an opportunity to buy. GNTX is a modestly decent growth stock that continues to be in a steadily growing industry (auto parts with 4.45% CAGR).

Forward-Looking Conclusion

Over the next five years, the analysts that follow this company are expecting it to grow earnings at an average annual rate of 14.01%.

In addition, the average one-year price target for this stock is at $31.87, which is about a 33% increase.

Here is an alternative scenario based on GNTX’s past earnings growth. During the past 10 and 5-year periods, the average EPS growth rate was about 5% and 4.53%, respectively.

But when considering cash flow growth over the past 10 and 5 years, the average growth has been 4.34% and -0.64%, respectively. Therefore, when considering all of these return possibilities, our average annual return could likely be around 10%.

If considering actual past results of Gentex Corp., the story is a bit different. Here are the actual 10 and 5-year return results.

______________

10 Year Return Results if Invested in GNTX:

Initial Investment Date: 4/4/2015

End Date: 4/4/2025

Cost per Share: $18

End Date Price: $22.04

Total Dividends Received: $4.30

Total Return: 46.33%

Compound Annualized Growth Rate: 4%

_______________

5 Year Return Results if Invested in GNTX:

Initial Investment Date: 4/4/2020

End Date: 4/4/2025

Cost per Share: $20.65

End Date Price: $21.92

Total Dividends Received: $2.40

Total Return: 17.77%

Compound Annualized Growth Rate: 3%

_________________

From these scenarios, we have produced results from 3% to 4%. These returns are smaller because the stock price has underperformed over the past year.

However, I feel that if you’re a long-term patient investor and believer in Gentex, and its existing products (automatic-dimming rearview mirrors and cameras for driver assistance systems), you could expect GNTX to provide you with around at least 10% annual return over the long haul. But for the short-term swing trader or impatient investor, the near future of GNTX, will likely show added volatility from auto industry tariffs, inflation, supply bottlenecks, and rising interest rates.

As a comparison, the S&P 500’s average return from 1928 – Present is about 10%. So, in a typical scenario with GNTX, you could expect to earn a similar long-term return as compared with an S&P 500 index fund. The individual GNTX stock obviously doesn’t offer the diversification that the index fund does.

Does Gentex Pass My Checklist?

  1. Company Rating 70+ out of 100? YES (84.75)
  2. Share Price Compound Annual Growth Rate > 12%? YES (10.32%)
  3. Earnings history mostly increasing? YES
  4. ROE (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (17.48%)
  5. ROIC (5-year average 16% or greater)? YES (17.48%)
  6. Gross Margin % (5-year average > 30%)? YES (33.98%)
  7. Debt-to-Equity (less than 1)? YES
  8. Current Ratio (greater than 1)? YES
  9. Outperformed S&P 500 during most of the past 10 years? YES
  10. Do I think this company will continue to successfully sell their same main product/service for the next 10 years? YES

Gentex scored 10/10 or 100%. Therefore, Gentex is definitely worth considering as a potential investment!

Is Gentex currently selling at a bargain price?

  1. Price Earnings less than 16? NO (13.3)
  2. Estimated Value greater than Current Stock Price? YES (Value $28.54 > $22.08 Stock Price).

Valuation metrics suggest that GNTX is selling at a bargain price as indicated by the Estimated Value, the Price Earnings Ratio, and my detailed Valuation Analysis.

With the recent underperformance of GNTX stock, it seems to be at an opportune price to buy. However, uncertainty surrounding President Trump’s tariff policies leaves this stock especially susceptible to more volatility. If you’re confident about this company and a long-term investor, then it seems like a reasonable time to buy. However, if you’re looking to invest with shorter holding period of 2 years or less, then you may consider waiting to see how the tariffs will affect Gentex specifically and to possibly buy at an even better price if the company stock declines more.
