I personally will add Gentex to my watch list and wait to see how the tariff situation unfolds and hope to buy at an even lower price.

data.title: President Trump Just Gave A Huge Gift To Dividend Investors
data.description:
The markets are crashing right now in response to President Donald Trump's April 2nd announcement of a dramatic change in U.S. trade policy. In particular, he levied huge tariffs in an effort to create a better balance in U.S. trade relations and ignite a boom in U.S. manufacturing. His "Liberation Day" initiative consisted of a universal 10% tariff levied on all imports, while additional tariffs are going to be levied on individual countries on a case-by-case basis. Some of the most dramatic tariff levels that his administration announced are 34% on China, 46% on Vietnam, 20% on the European Union, and 24% on Japan.

Already, China has levied massive reciprocal tariffs of its own at the same level that the United States is putting on them, while also speaking out strongly against U.S. actions. Meanwhile, several European leaders have spoken out strongly against the tariffs and are plotting their response, while Japan and other trading partners are considering countermeasures as well. These tariffs are causing the markets to crash due to expectations that they will lead to disruption in global trade patterns, a sharp reduction in profits for many companies that profit from the existing trade order, and there are growing concerns that a trade war will result in a severe recession.

In fact, JP Morgan (JPM) currently sees a 60% chance of recession if Trump's tariffs remain in place. As a result, it should not be surprising to see the Nasdaq (QQQ) now in bear market territory with leading mega-cap tech stocks like Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), and Meta (META) getting hammered, while also the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) and S&P 500 (SPY) are in free fall, as many other companies are seeing their stock prices get hit.

With that being said, we think that the market's selloff has been indiscriminate. As a result, dividend investors have a huge opportunity to lock in very attractive total returns from stocks of companies that are actually likely to benefit almost as much, if not more, than they will be hurt by these tariffs. In this article, we will detail two names that we think are likely to profit immensely from this trade war.

The Impact Of The Trade War On Interest Rates And Inflation

Before we get into the names, though, it's important to keep in mind how this tariff announcement is likely to impact the outlook for inflation and interest rates, which are the two main factors that impact most high-yielding dividend stocks. First and foremost, the 10-year yield on the U.S. Treasuries has fallen dramatically and now is below 4% for the first time in a while. This is due to fears of a recession and is a very positive tailwind for dividend stocks.

In fact, bond proxies like Realty Income (O), which may have some counterparties that suffer from tariffs, has largely been unaffected by the tariffs, as it is only down by about 1.6% over the past week. This is because falling bond yields offset any perceived increased risk due to tariffs, thereby making its bond-like triple net lease cash flows more attractive on a net present value basis. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve recently stated that it is likely going to hold the line on interest rates in the near term and views accelerating inflation due to tariffs as a bigger risk than rising unemployment at the moment. As a result, it is going to stand pat until it gets more data before making any changes to interest rates. However, the market thinks that the Federal Reserve is likely going to cut rates more aggressively than they would have otherwise, as they foresee a growing risk of an economic downturn due to the tariffs. As a result, traders are betting that the Fed will cut rates at least four times this year to bail out the economy. Meanwhile, family offices paused deal-making in March due to fears of a trade war as they want more clarity before proceeding with deal-making. All this points to is an environment where we're likely going to see stagflation, namely, a slow or even declining economy where interest rates are likely to fall, although on a limited scale, due to inflation likely remaining persistent at least for the short term due to tariff measures (though inflation likely will not get out of control either due to falling energy prices) and slowing economic activity in general. As a result, we think that the most opportunistic stocks to buy right now are ones that are recession and inflation-resistant and will benefit from falling interest rates, yet have also pulled back sharply in price recently due to the market's indiscriminate selling.

2 Top Picks On The Dip

Two names that I think are really attractive right now are Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) and Energy Transfer (ET), as they have sold off heavily despite standing to benefit at least somewhat from the side effects of the ongoing trade war. ET is down by about 15% and ARE is down by nearly 19% as I write this.

These businesses are both in excellent position to benefit from these developments because one of the big hits on ARE has been the rise in interest rates in recent years which has pushed down its stock price sharply and pushed up its dividend yield. This is due to the fact that ARE's yield had been fairly low and a lot of its properties had relatively low cap rates and so rising interest rates made them relatively less attractive. Yes, there are also headwinds facing the life sciences space due to the large conversion of empty office space into life science properties and this has weighed on ARE's growth.

However, ARE is still competitively positioned against these headwinds due to its well-located mega-cluster properties that are holding up very well on an occupancy basis. It has also continued to generate solid AFFO per share growth while also being able to grow its dividend and sustaining an extremely strong BBB plus credit rating with its fairly long-term lease income from competitively positioned properties and some high-quality counterparties. ARE should be quite recession resistant especially because life sciences and health care tend to be a fairly recession resistant industry. Meanwhile, it will benefit from declining interest rates as its properties and dividend yield should be considered more attractive on a net present value basis. Last, but not least, after the recent sell-off, ARE now trades at a near 60% discount to its analyst consensus estimate of NAV and its dividend yield is well above 6%, even as analysts expect its AFFO per share to grow at a 5.6% CAGR through 2029:

Meanwhile, ET is even is also likely to benefit from this scenario. While the pullback sharp decline in its unit price is understandable given how much energy prices oil prices have crashed and energy stocks like Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), and others have all pulled back sharply, and its LNG export business could suffer some from the trade war, there are also tailwinds to keep in mind. First of all, ET is largely resistant to commodity price volatility, as only ~10% of its EBITDA is exposed to this.

This means that its cash flow should hold up quite well despite energy prices falling and its very attractive distribution that now yields over 8% is covered about two times with distributable cash flow. As a result, it should be quite safe and should continue to grow at an inflation-beating pace moving forward.

On top of that, it does have some inflation-linked escalators in its pipeline contracts that mean that if we are in a position period of stagflation, its organic cash flows should remain quite strong. On top of that, it has a strong BBB credit rating and a freshly deleveraged balance sheet that should hold up quite well at the same time though. Falling interest rates also benefit ET because its cash flow stream is bond-like in nature too due to the long-term contracted nature of its pipeline contracts and it does have a steady stream of debt maturities in the years to come that will now be able to refinance at lower rates than it would have otherwise had the 10-year treasury not pulled back so sharply.

Investor Takeaway

I think both ARE and ET are being irrationally sold off by Mr. Market, as they now present dividend investors with an incredible opportunity to lock in high current yields along with inflation-beating dividend and cash flow growth potential for years to come. On top of that, they actually stand to benefit from some of the secondary impacts of these tariffs - namely falling interest rates - while being able to withstand a potential stagflationary environment quite well. Stocks with similar profiles as these, but which are even better bargains than ARE and ET, are the kinds of investments we are targeting in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Buy Gold, Emerging Markets, Value, And High Yields Now. The 'Mag 7', S&P 500 And QQQ Have Broken.
data.description:
The S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, and Magnificent 7 stocks peaked in the first quarter of 2025. Simultaneously, foreign and emerging market stocks have turned up as did gold and value stocks reflecting a leadership rotation out of overvalued and over owned mega caps and into long dormant geographies and sectors.

Through March 28, the table below shows the negative performance in the S&P 500, Dow Jones Industrial Index and NASDAQ as well as the positive performance in the MCSI EAFE and Emerging Markets, as well as Russell 1000 value indices. After decades of US market leadership and “American Exceptionalism”, we believe we are in a decade long leadership rotation similar to that which followed the 2000 market peak.

The first quarter experienced unusual uncertainty due to the Administration’s threatened tariff regime. This has paused business momentum but promises to bring manufacturing back to the US following decades of offshoring.

Continued fighting in Gaza and Ukraine also threaten wider wars and additional global economic instability. As the new administration seeks to implement meaningful policy changes in geopolitics, international trade, government efficiency, and safety, markets will likely tread water until policy implementation is clear. During this pause former leaders will lose momentum and new investment leadership should emerge or gain momentum.

Because momentum investing and cycle changes can meaningfully impact investment returns and portfolio strategy, this letter will seek to illuminate these important cycle and momentum changes.

Treasury Yields:

In 2020, 10-year US Treasury yields bottomed at 0.55% down from over 15% in 1981. With the advent of inflation in 2021, a four decade - long period of declining interest rates and inflation ended, and an inflationary cycle commenced. This four decade period of declining interest rates helped US equities, bonds and real estate, generate exceptional double-digit returns, a phenomenon that gave birth to the concept of “American Exceptionalism”. As stocks, bonds, and real estate stop delivering exceptional returns, we anticipate new sectors, assets, and geographies will become new investment leaders.

The chart of the 10-year US treasury yield below shows a reversal upwards in interest rates in 2020 and that interest rates are close to historically normal levels. Consequently, we anticipate interest rates will remain range bound and are not trending meaningfully lower.

The Inflation Cycle:

The chart below shows inflationary and deflationary cycles since 1887. We believe we are entering an inflationary cycle where the S&P 500 will underperform inflation and commodities. The 1970s and the 2000-2009 period are inflationary cycles and market analogs which highlight potential future leadership in commodities and emerging markets.

The Growth to Value Shift:

During declining interest rate environments long duration assets like growth and technology stocks appreciate disproportionately. That trend has reversed and the ratio of value to growth stocks should favor value stocks for the next several years. The chart below of the MSCI Growth/Value ratio suggests a period of protracted weakness in growth stocks versus value stocks lies ahead.

S&P 500 Near Peak Valuations:

The current Fed Risk Premium Model shows the stock market relative to the 10-year US Treasury yield is now in the red zone or below zero which was last experienced during the 2000 Technology market bubble.

A Cautionary Technical Indicator:

During the first quarter, the S&P 500 broke the 200-day moving average. While this break does not mean the market will sharply decline, all major market declines experience the S&P 500 price breaking below its 200-day moving average.

Parabolic Moves and Irrational Exuberance:

Nobel Laureate Robert Shiller’s book Irrational Exuberance showed the history of manias being correlated with historic over-valuations, parabolic stock charts, and irrational exuberance. The charts below from Crescat Capital capture the eerily similar parabolic rise of mega cap stocks today to that which was experienced during the 2000 Technology Bubble.

The Case for International Markets:

In the aftermath of the 2000 bubble, emerging markets became sources of meaningful equity market outperformance. From 2000 to 2007, the Chinese FXI rose nearly seven-fold, which was a remarkable outperformance. In the last few years, we have seen India and Argentina produce outsized returns. However, the chart below of CAPE ratios points to where potentially significant outperformance can be found in countries with very low CAPE Ratios. The chart below not only illustrates the extreme valuation risk to the Magnificent 7, India and the US, it also shows some extremely low valuations for Argentina, Brazil, Hong Kong, South Korea, and China. These countries appear to be regions where significant non-correlated alpha can be captured. With a successful end to the Russian war on Ukraine, significant opportunities could also be found in Europe.

Julian Bridgen of Macro Intelligence Partners 2 charted the protracted period of out performance of the MSCI emerging markets in the wake of the 2000 Technology bubble. We believe this illustrates a valuable historic analog for where geographical outperformance can be secured in the years ahead.

Likewise, Bridgen also charted sector outperformance in the post 2000 market bubble in the chart below, showing leadership in precious metals, energy, materials, mining and industrials. We forecast an outperformance in gold, silver and mining shares in the years ahead, but are less optimistic about energy stocks like oil and gas.

In the years ahead, as the US seeks to expand its oil and gas production, lower oil and gas prices will hurt the bottom line of oil and gas producers. Ironically, natural gas and oil producers did especially well during the Biden Administration as it was unfriendly to hydrocarbon development and prices rose, helping those producers’ earnings. However, the current administration would like lower oil and gas prices to reduce inflation and improve US consumer purchasing power – which in turn would be an economic headwind for oil and gas producers in the US. Lower oil prices could, however, hurt regimes unfavorable to the US like Russia, Iran, and Venezuela.

The Case for Gold Stocks:

We are especially sanguine about gold and gold stocks. Gold appreciated sharply during the 2000 to 2011 post bubble period from $282/oz to $1823/oz -- 546%. Today gold is benefitting from foreign central banks acquiring gold, like the Bank of China, safe haven investing, and de-dollarization, where the BRICS are looking to weaken the US dollar by trading in commodities and non-dollar currencies. A weaker dollar should drive higher gold prices in the years ahead. Furthermore, gold stocks are especially attractive as the value of these miner’s assets (GOLD) has tripled since 2015. See chart below.

Gold miner performance is shown in the chart below, with the metal represented by the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) candlestick chart and gold miners represented the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) by the thin rose line and the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) by the thin green line. Gold stocks historically track earnings and earnings surprises. With the rise in the price of gold, earnings should predictably rise and drive gold mining stocks in the coming quarters, while most other equities are faced with uncertainty.

To illustrate how cheap junior gold miners are today, despite the rise in the price of gold since 2011, the VanEck Junior Gold Miner ETF is one third of where it peaked in 2011. It seems reasonable that the VanEck Junior Gold Miner ETF should experience a tripling to recapture its 2011 highs. If a protracted period of economic instability, inflation, and de-dollarization persists, even greater upside could be realized by junior gold miners.

Tavi Costa at Crescat Capital charted just how dramatically cash flows are rising relative to the Philadelphia Gold and Silver Exchange Index (XAU) in the chart below. A 50% rise in the index would seem appropriate, based on the forward two-year cash flow estimate for the XAU. That upward momentum in gold stock shares should feed on itself, like any persistent market trend.

Alternative to a 60% Stock 40% Bond Portfolio:

With these investment themes in mind, we constructed a $2 million portfolio for a retired investor interested in gold stocks, diversification, and about 5% income. The portfolio utilizes several closed end equity and bond funds as well as high-yielding international stocks and yields 7.73%. The takeaway is that years of underperformance in international and emerging markets has created yield opportunities that are diversified and uncorrelated to a 60% cap weighted equity, 40% bond portfolio.

Recency Bias

Recency bias is the psychological investment phenomenon where people overweight recent experience and excessively discount the long term historical record. We believe that the recent rise in interest rates will lead investors to unwittingly believe that interest rates will trend lower. This favorable interest rate bias will also mislead investors into believing that great returns will continue to come from stocks, bonds, and real estate, but their historic double-digit returns have been the result of declining interest rates that are unlikely to persist.

Conclusion:

The resurgence of inflation and rise in interest rates, since 2020, has fundamentally changed the return prospects for stocks, bonds, and real estate for the next decade. The nearly four decades of declining rates from 1981 to 2020 provided a powerful fundamental factor and valuation driver for equities, bonds, and real estate.

The next decade is unlikely to enjoy declining interest rates and declining inflation. This shift in fundamental factors underscores the inflationary and deflationary cycles since 1887 presented earlier. As a result of the parabolic boom in US equities, new leadership will likely come from other assets and geographies. International and emerging market stocks and commodities and commodity stocks are likely to be the sources of new leadership. In the years ahead, double-digit returns will most likely be found in assets classes or industries like precious metals mining in the years ahead. In addition, potentially great returns are likely to be found in emerging markets like Argentina, Peru, Hungary, Hong Kong, South Korea and potentially Russia and Ukraine if peace is found there.
